summary: Jeff Atwood explores Edward Tufte's observations about the vast resolution gap between printed pages and computer displays. He demonstrates this by scanning sparklines from Tufte's 'Beautiful Evidence' at various resolutions, showing how printed material at 600-2400 DPI dwarfs the typical 72-100 DPI of computer monitors. The post serves as a practical illustration of why print media can achieve far greater information density than screens. Even cheap printers dramatically outperform the most expensive displays of the era.
key_points: Commercial printing operates at around 2400 DPI, while typical computer displays only achieve 72-100 DPI
Sparklines are a Tufte technique specifically designed to exploit print's superior resolution
Even a 7-year-old 600 DPI printer produces vastly sharper output than contemporary high-end monitors
The resolution gap explains why printed pages can achieve greater information density than screens
money_quotes: A recurring theme in Edward Tufte's books is the massive difference in resolution between the printed page and computer displays.
Printed pages lend themselves to vastly greater information density.
The difference in resolution between the most expensive computer display you can buy and a cheap off-brand printer really is astronomical.
We have a long, long way to go before computer displays can get anywhere near printer resolutions.
key_insight: The resolution gap between print and screen isn't just noticeable—it's so vast that even obsolete printers dramatically outperform cutting-edge displays, fundamentally limiting what we can accomplish with on-screen information design.
